paper_title: AFFORDANCE20Q: Evaluating Affordance Reasoning from Physical Properties

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Affordance reasoning, the inference of an object's action possibilities from its physical properties (e.g., shape and material), is fundamental to human physical understanding and increasingly critical for Large Language Models (LLMs). However, existing affordance benchmarks largely expose explicit object identities in the evaluation setup, allowing models to rely on memorized object-affordance mappings rather than reasoning over physical properties. To address this gap, we introduce Affordance20Q, a novel affordance reasoning benchmark formulated as a 20-Questions game without exposing the object's identity. In each game, the model identifies a hidden object's affordance from a candidate set by asking yes/no questions about its physical properties. Affordance20Q comprises 1,009 games over 454 objects and 59 affordances, all manually filtered, refined, and annotated. We conduct comprehensive experiments with 15 state-of-the-art LLMs and find a substantial gap (~20 points) compared to human performance. A KL-based information-gain (IG) analysis further shows that models fail to ask discriminating questions as the game progresses. To close the gap, we develop KB-Anchored Rule Induction (KARI), a pipeline based on LLMs that generates affordance rules grounded in evidence from knowledge bases (KBs). KARI improves open-source LLMs by up to 15.2 points, while the limited coverage of KBs hinders further gains. We release all our code and data at https://github.com/1171-jpg/Affordance20Q.git

sections.title: Start
sections.content: AFFORDANCE20Q: Evaluating Affordance Reasoning from Physical Properties Yifan Jiang1, Meige Yang2, Zitong Li2, Jay Pujara1 1Information Sciences Institute, University of Southern California 2University of Southern California {yifjia,jpujara}@isi.edu, maggieya@usc.edu, alex.zitong.li@gmail.com 6 2 0 2 2 1 ] A . [ 1 0 4 2 4 1 . 6 0 6 2 : r a

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Affordance reasoning, the inference of an objects action possibilities from its physical properties (e.g., shape and material), is fundamental to human physical understanding and increasingly critical for Large Language Models (LLMs). However, existing affordance benchmarks largely expose explicit object identities in the evaluation setup, allowing models to rely on memorized object-affordance mappings rather than reasoning over physical properties. To address this gap, we introduce AFFORDANCE20Q, novel affordance reasoning benchmark formulated as 20-Questions game without exposing the objects identity. In each game, the model identifies hidden objects affordance from candidate set by asking yes/no questions about its physical properties. AFFORDANCE20Q comprises 1,009 games over 454 objects and 59 affordances, all manually filtered, refined, and annotated. We conduct comprehensive experiments with 15 state-of-the-art LLMs and find substantial gap (20 points) compared to human performance. KL-based information-gain (IG) analysis further shows that models fail to ask discriminating questions as the game progresses. To close the gap, we develop KB-Anchored Rule Induction (KARI), pipeline based on LLMs that generates affordance rules grounded in evidence from knowledge bases (KBs). KARI improves open-source LLMs by up to 15.2 points, while the limited coverage of KBs hinders further gains. We release all our code and data at https://github. com/1171-jpg/Affordance20Q.git

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: When humans encounter an object, they reason about what actions it supports directly from its physical properties, including its shape, material, and structure. Gibson (1977) formalized this capacity as affordance reasoning and placed it at the core of physical understanding. Affordance reasoning operates on physical properties that every 1 Figure 1: Comparison between existing affordance benchmarks (top) and AFFORDANCE20Q (bottom). object exhibits, and therefore supports wide range of physical interaction, from fluently using familiar everyday objects (Norman, 2013) to robustly handle novel ones or creatively repurposing familiar ones beyond their typical use (Duncker and Lees, 1945; German and Defeyter, 2000). Such reasoning is increasingly crucial for Large Language Models (LLMs) as recent progress has led to their integration into daily human activities (Yang et al., 2025; Singh et al., 2025), and especially for the growing class of LLM-driven embodied systems (Driess et al., 2023; Zhang et al., 2025) that aim for physical application. LLMs are expected not only to understand everyday tool use (Wang et al., 2023b, 2026b), but also to generalize this capability to unfamiliar objects or scenarios beyond their training experience (Tian et al., 2024; Jiang et al., 2023b). For example, in embodied robotic manipulation, model should not only recognize that knife affords cutting, but also reason that any object with sharp rigid edge affords the same action (Tang et al., 2025; Xu et al., 2022). Recognizing its importance, recent research has introduced diverse benchmarks for affordance reasoning, spanning multiple modalities (Wang et al., 2026b; Yu et al., 2025) and task types (Qasemi et al., 2022; Tian et al., 2024). Yet all setups make the objects identity explicit by giving the class or object name (Figure 1), conflating recall of object-affordance mappings with reasoning from physical properties. model knowing that the object is knife can answer it affords cutting simply by recalling stored mapping (Persiani and Hellström, 2019), without ever consulting its rigid, sharp-edged physical properties. Such conflation prevents existing benchmarks from accurately measuring affordance reasoning capability. Recallbased models can pass these benchmarks within the training distribution, but fail on the unseen objects and atypical uses common in real-world physical interaction (Gjerde et al., 2025; Wu et al., 2024). To disentangle reasoning from recall, we introduce AFFORDANCE20Q, benchmark cast in 20Questions (20Q) game setting (Bruner et al., 1966), in which models identify an objects affordance without knowing its identity. As shown in Figure 1, in each game, given candidate affordances, the model narrows the candidates through multi-turn yes/no questions about the hidden objects physical properties, such as material and shape. The exclusion of object identity requires the model to identify the correct affordance by reasoning from physical evidence rather than recalling stored objectaffordance mappings (Hutson et al., 2025). To construct AFFORDANCE20Q, we use three-stage pipeline that first collects physical objects and affordances from the existing corpus (Jiang and Riloff, 2021) and commonsense knowledge base (Ilievski et al., 2021), then enriches them via LLM generation, and finally manually filters, refines, and annotates (object, affordance) pairs. In the end, AFFORDANCE20Q consists of 1,009 games covering 454 objects and 59 affordances. With AFFORDANCE20Q, we evaluate 15 stateof-the-art LLMs with different sizes and architectures, and find substantial gap (20 points) compared to human performance, with the strongest model reaching only 45.9%. To further diagnose the gap, we trace each game with KL-based information-gain (IG) metric and find that models repeatedly ask low-IG questions that fail to narrow the candidate set across turns. To close this gap, we develop KB-Anchored Rule Induction (KARI), pipeline that uses knowledge bases to both inspire LLM rule generation and re-ground the generated rules through post-hoc validation, ensuring that generated rules remain anchored in physical commonsense rather than free-form LLM speculation. KARI improves open-source LLMs by up to 15.2 points, partially closing the gap, while the remaining shortfall traces to the coverage limits of current commonsense knowledge bases. We summarize our contributions as follows: 1) We introduce AFFORDANCE20Q, 20-Questions benchmark that tests affordance reasoning from physical properties rather than object-identity recall, comprising 1,009 games over 454 objects and 59 affordances. 2) We conduct comprehensive experiments with 15 state-of-the-art LLMs, revealing substantial gap to human performance, with information-gain analysis showing that models fail to ask discriminating questions as the game progresses. 3) We develop KB-Anchored Rule Induction (KARI), pipeline that combines LLMs and knowledge bases, improving open-source LLMs by up to 15.2 points. We release all code and data.

sections.title: 2 Related Work
sections.content: Affordance Reasoning Benchmarks The importance of affordance reasoning (Gibson, 1977) has motivated wide range of benchmarks across diverse input formats and modalities. In the vision domain, early work focuses on grounding part/objectlevel affordance in either 3D shape and part geometry (Deng et al., 2021; Xu et al., 2022) or 2D image input (Nguyen et al., 2017; Luo et al., 2022; Li et al., 2023). Recent work shifts to evaluating models affordance reasoning with both image inputs and text instructions across different task setups (Wang et al., 2026b; Yu et al., 2025; Wang et al., 2026a; Zhu et al., 2025; Wan et al., 2025). In the text domain, parallel work formats affordance reasoning as question-answering tasks with object names or descriptions provided as context (Bisk et al., 2020; Aroca-Ouellette et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2023b; Adak et al., 2024; Gjerde et al., 2025). Although few works (Li et al., 2023; Gjerde et al., 2025) format affordance reasoning based on physical properties, none of them remove object identity to prevent recall of object-affordance mappings. In contrast, AFFORDANCE20Q is the first benchmark to exclude object identity from the input and require the model to infer affordance from physical properties through multi-turn questioning, disentangling reasoning from recall. 2 20-Questions Games and Active QuestionAsking The 20-Questions (20Q) game was first used in cognitive science to study informationseeking behavior (Bruner et al., 1966; Ruggeri In each game, questioner aims et al., 2016). to identify hidden target through sequence of yes/no questions within fixed number of turns. As active question-asking ability becomes increasingly important in real-world human-computer interaction scenarios (e.g., task disambiguation (Kobalczyk et al., 2025), medical diagnosis (Li et al., 2024)), the 20Q game has recently been widely adopted to analyze LLMs multi-turn reasoning and information-seeking abilities (Bertolazzi et al., 2023; Hutson et al., 2025; Zhang et al., 2024; Mazzaccara et al., 2024). However, all current work chooses the object or entity as the candidate space. For example, Zhang et al. (2024) and Hutson et al. (2025) require LLMs to strategically ask questions to identify hidden target object. AFFORDANCE20Q is the first to adopt the 20Q game for affordance reasoning, which also aligns with Gibsons active-perception view of affordance (Gibson, 1977). We further introduce novel KL-based information-gain metric to evaluate model question effectiveness across turns.

sections.title: 3 AFFORDANCE20Q Construction
sections.content: In this section, we first formalize the game setup of AFFORDANCE20Q (3.1), then describe the design of our three-stage collection pipeline (3.2), and end up with the data statistics (3.3).

sections.title: 3.1 Game Formulation
sections.content: Following recent 20Q adaptations for LLM evaluation (Hutson et al., 2025; Zhang et al., 2024), each game in AFFORDANCE20Q is defined by hidden target object and candidate affordance set = {a, a1, . . . , a7} of 8 affordances, in which the target is an affordance that possesses and the remaining 7 are distractors. Three agents participate: Questioner that observes only and asks yes/no questions (q) about os physical properties to identify a, Checker that ensures each question is both well-formed and grounded in one of the physical-property dimensions (e.g., material, shape), preventing information leakage, and an Oracle that has access to and provides answers (r) to physical-property questions. At each turn t, the Questioner produces question qt based on the dialogue history Ht1 = {(q1, r1), . . . , (qt1, rt1)}, the Checker validates qt, and the Oracle returns an answer rt. The game succeeds if the Questioner correctly identifies within budget of = 20 turns. It fails if the Questioner makes an incorrect guess or exhausts the turn budget.

sections.title: 3.2 Three-Stage Collection Pipeline
sections.content: Unlike previous 20Q setups (Hutson et al., 2025; Zhang et al., 2024) that frame the task as identifying hidden object, to evaluate affordance reasoning, AFFORDANCE20Q frames it as identifying hidden objects affordance, where the hidden object remains but the Questioner reasons over its physical properties. To ensure this reasoning chain is valid, AFFORDANCE20Q excludes any objectaffordance pair whose affordance is not deducible from physical-property dimensions alone (e.g., microwaves heating affordance comes from its magnetron). Given the cost of fully manual curation, we design semi-automatic three-stage collection pipeline to construct AFFORDANCE20Q in scalable way. Stage 1: Initial Object and Affordance Collection. Our initial object pool is the list of human-made physical objects introduced in (Jiang and Riloff, 2021). We build the initial affordance pool by querying Commonsense Knowledge Graph (CSKG) (Ilievski et al., 2021) for the relations describing what objects are capable of and used for (e.g., CapableOf, UsedFor). CSKG integrates seven commonsense knowledge bases (e.g., ConceptNet (Speer et al., 2017), WordNet (Fellbaum, 2010)) into one schema and thus offers denser affordance coverage than any single source. Stage 2: Affordance Expansion and Pool Filtering. To further expand the affordance coverage, we query CSKG for each objects physical properties and prompt an LLM to propose additional candidate affordances conditioned on them. To remove entries unsuitable for our task, we then manually filter both pools based on specific principles. For affordances, we remove entries that violate the following three dimensions: (1) contextdependence, the affordance depends on external context rather than the object itself (e.g., being gift), (2) mechanism-dependence, the affordance depends on hidden internal mechanisms (e.g., playing video), and (3) over-generality, the affordance is too abstract to be discriminative (e.g., useful). For objects, we drop entries that are not discrete physical objects (e.g., raw material, human body part) or whose function depends on hidden internal 3 mechanisms (e.g., microwave, TV). After filtering, we refine affordance names and write short description for each, and consolidate each objects CSKG properties by removing irrelevant or conflicting entries, forming each objects property set for Stage 3 (Details in Section D). Stage 3: Annotation and Object Specialization. In this stage, we annotate every (object, affordance) pair, determining whether each affordance applies to its paired object. Additionally, as each objects property set from CSKG tends to be generic and lacks specific physical details (e.g., some spatulas have an internal hollow bar that filters water), we also specialize each objects property set during annotation. We first prompt an LLM to label every (object, affordance) pair as YES, NO, or MAYBE given the objects property set and the affordances definition, naming the additional property required for each MAYBE label. We then manually verify each YES/NO label against the objects property set, and for each MAYBE, decide whether the proposed property applies, and if it does, add the property to the set, turn the label to YES, and re-check consistency. Figure 2: tion (KARI) Illustration of KB-Anchored Rule Inducare both grounded and expressive, thereby assisting the Questioner in our task. Therefore, we develop KB-Anchored Rule Induction (KARI), pipeline that uses an LLM to induce compositional rule per affordance grounded in evidence from external KBs. We next describe KARIs rule format and how its components combine KB knowledge with LLM reasoning during rule generation. 3.

sections.title: 4.1 Rule Format
sections.content: We run the pipeline with GPT-4.1 (OpenAI, 2023) as the main LLM due to its promising performance in public benchmarks (Liang et al., 2022). Six human annotators were involved in the manual annotation and refinement process in Stages 2 and 3. We further recruited three additional annotators to re-annotate random subset of 1,298 pairs, achieving an 85.2% majority agreement with our released labels (Fleiss κ = 0.82 (Fleiss, 1971)). Applying the three-stage pipeline yields 454 objects, each with property set, 59 affordances, and label for every (object, affordance) pair. We sample 1,009 (object, target affordance) game instances as the test set, balanced across target affordances so that no single affordance dominates, forming AFFORDANCE20Q.

sections.title: 4 KB-Anchored Rule Induction
sections.content: Prior work on affordance and commonsense reasoning either distills rules from KBs bound by fixed schema (Zhu et al., 2014), or generates them with LLMs but risks hallucination (West et al., 2022). We investigate whether combining KB knowledge with LLM reasoning yields rules that KARI rule is tree expression stating the static physical conditions enabling an affordance. KARIs rule grammar has seven operators in three categories. (i) Four atomic predicates describe physical property of the entity: MADE_OF, SHAPE, SIZE, and SURFACE. Each takes value to represent one physical property. For instance, MADE_OF: metal asserts that the entity is made of metal. (ii) Two combinators produce combination of multiple atomic predicates: AND and OR, which can nest inside each other. For instance, AND(MADE_OF: metal, SHAPE: pointed) requires the entity to be both metal and pointed. (iii) The constructor PART(x, predicate/combination) represents specific part of the object, where is variable that can refer to any part. We introduce PART because many affordances can be enabled by one specific part rather than the entire object, and we use additional part variables (e.g., y, z) when multiple distinct parts are required.

sections.title: 4.2 Rule Proposer, Validator, and Auditor
sections.content: KARI consists of three components, each for specific purpose (Figure 2). We first introduce each component, then illustrate the overall KARI 4 pipeline. We collect affordance verbs1 and corresponding positive objects together with their materials, parts, and other physical properties from CSKG (Ilievski et al., 2021) and the Aristo Tuple KB (Dalvi et al., 2017) as our initial data. Proposer. The proposer is the only component that writes rule expressions. Given an affordance verb and set of objects with their physical properties from knowledge base, it either drafts new rule or extends an existing one by appending new OR branch. We restrict the proposers behavior to be add-only to ensure possible errors will only occur in the new branch. Validator. The validator takes an object and set of rules as input. Since the object is positive example of the affordance verb according to the KB, each rule is expected to evaluate true on it. For each rule, an LLM labels every atom as YES or NO, and code script evaluates the AND/OR tree to determine whether the rule covers the object. Auditor. The auditor validates each value the proposer introduces and maintains vocabulary of accepted values per predicate, which prevents illtyped values from entering the rule and the vocabulary from drifting through redundant synonyms. Given rule, the auditor checks whether each value is valid for its predicate (e.g., MADE_OF: cylinder is rejected), then either maps the value to an existing synonym in the vocabulary or adds it as new entry for future use.

sections.title: 4.3 Pipeline
sections.content: Figure 2 shows the overview of the pipeline. We split the collected data into three groups and call each component in loop. In round 1, the proposer drafts seed rule on the first group of data, and the auditor updates the values and the vocabulary. In rounds 2 and 3, the validator first validates the previous rule, and the proposer proposes new rule for uncovered objects in the new group, with the auditor performing the same update again. Adapting KARI to our collected data with QWEN3-14B for all components produces 2,223 rules. In the inference time, for each game, we compute the sentence similarity (Ni et al., 2022) between each candidate affordance and every generated rules affordance verb, and match the candidate to its highest-scoring rule when the similarity exceeds 0.7. Matched rules are verbalized at the 1We use affordance verb to refer to affordances collected from KBs in the KARI pipeline. end of the Questioners system prompt. candidate affordance with no match above the threshold receives no rule. Full details and rule examples are presented in Section A.

sections.title: 5 Experiment Setup
sections.content: We evaluate on the 1,009-game test split of AFFORDANCE20Q(Section 3), with the human baseline run on sampled subset. The Questioner, Oracle, and Checker all run at temperature 0. Full prompts and other details are in Section B.

sections.title: 5.1 Questioners
sections.content: To ensure comprehensive evaluation, we test 15 LLMs as Questioners, grouped into open-source and closed-source models. Open-source LLMs. We evaluate ten opensource models spanning wide range of scales. Eight are dense models between 8B and 14B parameters: QWEN3-8/14B and QWEN3.59B (Yang et al., 2025), PHI-4-14B (Abdin et al., 2024), LLAMA-3.1-8B (Grattafiori et al., 2024), MINISTRAL-8B (Liu et al., 2026), NEMOTRON9B (Blakeman et al., 2025), and GEMMA-312B (Team et al., 2025). The other two are the mixture-of-experts models DEEPSEEK-V4-PRO and DEEPSEEK-V4-FLASH (DeepSeek-AI, 2026). Closed-source LLMs. We evaluate five proprietary models accessed through their official APIs: GPT-5 and GPT-5-MINI (Singh et al., 2025), GEMINI-2.5-PRO and GEMINI-2.5-FLASH (Comanici et al., 2025), and MINIMAX-M2.5 (MiniMax, 2025). Reference points. 1) HUMAN: five annotators play 30% subset under the same game rules, and we report the average result. We further include two non-LLM reference points to show the possible performance range. To enable the final guess, for each candidate affordance, we select ten representative objects, and use the fraction of them that remain consistent with all questions and their corresponding Oracle answers. We then softmax these fractions across affordances as the likelihood of being the target. 2) FIX20Q asks the 20 most frequent questions from the LLM evaluation in fixed order (Appendix B.1), then samples its final guess from softmax over these likelihoods, and we report the average over five runs. 3) OPTIMAL is an information-theoretic upper bound with full access to all candidates representative objects. At 5 every turn, it asks the question that drives the target affordances likelihood as high as possible, and gives the final answer once single affordances likelihood exceeds specific threshold (0.9).

sections.title: 5.2 Oracle and Checker
sections.content: We use single QWEN3-14B instance for both the Oracle and the Checker. Since we provide each objects property set (Section 3.2) as context, it attains acceptable accuracy, which we confirm by manual verification (Section B.4).

sections.title: 5.3 Metrics
sections.content: We report three metrics: 1) Success Rate (SR), the fraction of games in which the target affordance is identified within the 20-turn budget, 2) Turns, the average number of turns in solved games, and 3) Information Gain (IG), per-turn measure of how fast each question narrows the candidate affordances. Using the same representative objects, let nt(a) be the number of affordance as representative objects still consistent with the dialogue history after turn t, giving distribution bt(a) = nt(a)/ (cid:80) nt(a) over the 8 candidates. IG is the KL divergence between consecutive distributions, IGt = DKL(bt bt1) = (cid:88) bt(a) log2 bt(a) bt1(a) . (1)

sections.title: 6 Results
sections.content: We focus on five research questions: 1) How well do LLMs reason about affordances from physical properties? 2) How do LLMs behave when reasoning about different affordances? 3) How effectively do LLMs gather information through questioning? 4) Can KARIs rules improve LLMs affordance reasoning? 5) What are the typical success and failure modes?

sections.title: 6.1 Main Results
sections.content: We report the success rate and average turns in Table 1. OPTIMAL reaches 100% in 2.5 turns, while FIX20Q reaches only 24.8% despite using the full 20-question budget, showing that extensive questioning without affordance reasoning specific to each game is not beneficial for success. Humans reach 64.2% in 10.7 turns, and their gap to OPTIMAL shows the challenging nature of AFFORDANCE20Q. We note that 2.5 turns is theoretical minimum, achievable only by optimally ordering questions to rule out distractor affordances or confirm the target affordance, whereas humans can ultimately solve the games in more turns, confirming the validity of AFFORDANCE20Q. For both openand closed-source LLMs, all models fall far short of human performance, with gaps ranging from roughly 20 to 50 points. Most open-source LLMs perform similarly to FIX20Q, ranging from 14.9% to 27.4%, showing they struggle to solve each game with appropriate affordance reasoning. DEEPSEEK-V4-FLASH and DEEPSEEK-V4-PRO are the clear exceptions, consistent with their strong results on other benchmarks (DeepSeek-AI, 2026) and likely aided by their mixture-of-experts architecture. All closedsource LLMs except GPT-5-MINI achieve higher success rates than the 8B to 14B models. Notably, GEMINI-2.5-PRO outperforms the strongest open-source LLM (DEEPSEEK-V4-FLASH) by 4.6 points, showing 4.6-point gap between the two groups best models. Models also differ in their turn usage, which does not guarantee higher success rate. For example, NEMOTRON-9B and PHI4-14B tend to end games early, while QWEN3.59B tends to exhaust its turn budget, but neither yields higher success rate.

sections.title: 6.2 Affordance Difficulty
sections.content: We further break down model performance by affordance (full results in Section C), which ranges widely from 3.1% to 56.0%. In general, affordances that can be inferred from single physical property tend to be easy, such as transmit_light (51.4%), established once the object is known to be transparent, or conduct_heat (55.2%), once it is known to be made of metal material. The hardest affordances instead require reasoning over multiple physical properties or alternative affordance rules, such as sink_in_water (3.8%), which jointly depends on the material of the object, whether it contains hollow structure inside, and whether the material can absorb the water, or hang_from_above (10.6%), where the object has hook-shaped, ring-shaped, or strap-like part that can support this affordance. We next compare openand closed-source LLMs performance on each affordance. Open-source LLMs often perform better when an affordance hinges on single physical property, while closed-source LLMs spend more turns yet result in lower success rate, suggesting potential overthinking behavior. On the other 6 Questioner Reference Points OPTIMAL HUMAN FIX20Q Open-source LLMs LLAMA-3.1-8B MINISTRAL-8B QWEN3-8B NEMOTRON-9B QWEN3.5-9B GEMMA-3-12B QWEN3-14B PHI-4-14B DEEPSEEK-V4-FLASH DEEPSEEK-V4-PRO Closed-source LLMs GPT-5-MINI GPT-5 MINIMAX-M2.5 GEMINI-2.5-FLASH GEMINI-2.5-PRO Vanilla +KARI SR Turns SR Turns 100.0 64.27.3 24.80.9 2.5 10.71.3 20.0 18.4 17.7 26.3 14.9 25.1 27.4 27.3 26.4 41.3 40.5 22.8 35.5 34.9 41.4 45.9 14.5 15.1 16.1 8.6 18.6 15.1 18.6 11.2 16.9 18. 16.9 14.5 17.2 17.3 18.5 31.312.9 28.010.3 33.47.1 30.015.1 33.28.1 33.76.3 32.95.6 30.54.1 37.93.4 38.52.0 31.28.4 32.43.1 35.50.6 33.48.0 40.05.9 20.0 12.6 11.7 17.4 14.3 18.6 10.9 11.9 10.6 11.2 10.9 7.0 9.3 9.9 12. Table 1: Main results on AFFORDANCE20Q, with and without KARIs rule integration. SR is the success rate (%), and Turns is the average turns. The best overall result is in bold, the best in each category underlined. hand, closed-source LLMs outperform open-source LLMs on affordances requiring multiple physical properties, with similar turn usage, highlighting their stronger reasoning ability. Figure 3: Question behavior (a) and information gain (b) over turns. physical-property dimensions and visualize their share at each turn in Figure 3(a). We observe clear trend: LLMs prefer to start the game with material question and later adopt shape questions to guide their decisions. Size and surface questions occupy only small share in the initial stage and grow afterward, while shape questions come to dominate the share (around 40%). We further examine whether this question behavior is meaningful by scoring each turn with its information gain (IG) (Figure 3(b)). We find that material and shape questions provide useful information gain in the initial turns (1 to 5), although the large gap from OPTIMAL shows these are still not the best questions to ask. The IG then collapses after turn 5 and gradually approaches zero, even though models keep asking questions from different categories. This shows that, in the early turns, LLMs can ask basic questions that roughly build up likelihoods over the candidate affordances to assist affordance reasoning. As the game proceeds, they cannot ask more discriminating questions needed to separate the target affordance from the remaining distractors, leaving them uncertain at the final guess. This also confirms our earlier observation that asking more questions does not improve the success rate.

sections.title:
We use the four atomic predicates in our KARI rule
system to categorize LLM questions into different
sections.content: The result of KARIs rule integration  (Table 1)  is mainly two-fold. For models with 8B to 14B pa7 Figure 4: Accuracy change split by the target affordance coverage. rameters, we observe clear improvement in success rate, ranging from 4.1 to 15.2 points, verifying the effectiveness of the generated rules. We also notice that the rules have mixed impact on the turn number for these models: huge decrease is observed for QWEN3-8B/14B, while NEMOTRON9B doubles its turns, and LLAMA-3.1-8B even uses all 20 turns. For most closed-source LLMs and DeepSeek-V4 series, we find that KARIs rules bring drop in both success rate and turns, suggesting the rules lead these LLMs to make fast but unsure final guesses. KARIs rules are generated from knowledge bases whose limited coverage is known limitation in prior work (Bosselut et al., 2019; Hwang et al., 2021). We note that only 67.4% of the games in AFFORDANCE20Q have their target affordance covered by KARI rule. We split games by this coverage to analyze whether the limited coverage is barrier to the improvement brought by KARIs rules (Figure 4). On covered target affordances, KARIs rules actually supply useful affordance knowledge and improve every LLM by 17.7 points on average. On uncovered ones, the distractor affordances rules mislead the LLM toward irrelevant properties and lower the success rate by 25.0 points, hurting the strongest LLMs most, which can already solve some of these games even without rules.

sections.title: 6.5 Case Study
sections.content: We present three games with the same game setting in Figure 5. The first two use DEEPSEEK-V4FLASH and MINIMAX-M2.5 as the Questioner; the third reuses DEEPSEEK-V4-FLASH assisted with KARIs rules. 2 In the first example, the model has already collected enough physical properties to support the target affordance pierce through early in the game (T1, T3), yet it continues asking until 2In the actual game, rules mapped to the other candidate affordances are also presented. We omit them here for space. 8 Figure 5: Success and failure games with the target affordance pierce through (hidden object: knife). the turn budget is exhausted. Although it eventually produces the correct final guess, the behavior suggests overthinking or low confidence in reasoning from physical properties to specific affordance. The second example further illustrates this pattern: the model gathers sufficient evidence for the target affordance (T1, T5) but still arrives at wrong final guess. In contrast, the third example shows that KARIs rule can provide explicit and effective affordance knowledge, allowing the same model to solve the game accurately in just two turns.

sections.title: 7 Conclusion
sections.content: We introduced AFFORDANCE20Q, 20-Questions benchmark that measures affordance reasoning from physical properties rather than object-identity recall, comprising 1,009 games over 454 objects and 59 affordances. Our experiments with 15 state-of-the-art LLMs reveal substantial gap (20 points) compared to human performance, and KLbased information-gain analysis shows that models fail to ask discriminating questions as the game progresses. To close the gap, we proposed KARI, pipeline based on LLMs that generates affordance rules grounded in evidence from knowledge bases. KARI improves open-source LLMs by up to 15.2 points, while the limited coverage of KBs hinders further gains. We release the benchmark and all code, and hope AFFORDANCE20Q drives progress on physical reasoning that generalizes beyond memorized object-affordance mappings.

sections.title: 8 Limitations
sections.content: of physical Scope affordances. AFFORDANCE20Q covers 59 affordances and 454 objects sampled from CSKG. However, we want to note that we filter out those affordances not deducible from physical-property dimensions alone. For example, bulb can provide light through electricity. Although some benchmarks (Wang et al., 2026b) make initial attempts in this direction, systematic study from non-physical properties to such affordances remains unexplored. Text-only domain. AFFORDANCE20Q is the first work to test models affordance reasoning ability from physical properties without exposing the objects identity. However, the whole benchmark is set up in text-only setting. Exploring similar setting in the visual domain can provide more insights into how current MLLMs perform on affordance reasoning, which also aligns with many real-world scenario tasks (Jiang et al., 2026; Driess et al., 2023; Zhang et al., 2025; Jiang et al., 2024). Question-Answering Setting. Our benchmark formulates affordance reasoning in questionanswering (QA) setting. Real-world applications often require models to reason about affordances embedded in richer contexts, such as narrative understanding (Jiang et al., 2023a; Koˇcisk`y et al., 2018) or open-ended planning (Wang et al., 2023a), where affordance cues are implicit and must be inferred from context rather than elicited through explicit queries. Extending AFFORDANCE20Q to these settings can be promising direction for future work.

sections.title: 9 Ethical Considerations
sections.content: Data sources. Our affordance vocabulary and positive evidence are drawn from CSKG (Ilievski et al., 2021), which is publicly released for research use. The benchmark contains only physicalproperty descriptions of everyday objects and includes no personal or sensitive data. Intended use and potential risks. AFFORDANCE20Q is intended for research on physical affordance reasoning. While prior work has shown can be exploited to elicit harmful behaviors from LLMs through multi-turn attacks (Jiang et al., 2025; Wang et al., 2025), our task is strictly confined to querying physical properties of everyday objects, 9 and thus the benchmark and KARI rules contain no offensive content. We note that closed-source LLM scores reported here may shift as providers update their underlying models.

sections.content: This material is based upon work supported by the Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency (DARPA) under Agreement No. HR00112590089. This research was sponsored by the Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency via Contract HR00112390061.

sections.title: A KARI Pipeline
sections.content: A.1 Prompts We list the system prompts for three components as follows: Proposer. The proposer writes the rule given the affordance verbs and corresponding positive objects with physical properties from KB. You are physical reasoning expert. Given an affordance verb and example objects that can perform it, produce rule describing the PHYSICAL PREREQUISITES. ## GRAMMAR (output valid JSON only) Logic (2) -- combine sub-expressions: {"op": "AND", "args": [expr, ...]} {"op": "OR", "args": [expr, ...]} Constructor (1) -- introduce named part of the object: {"op": "PART", "role": "<var>", "body": expr} Atomic predicates (4) -- attach static physical property: {"op": "MADE_OF", "role": "<wholevar>", " class": "<material>"} {"op": "SHAPE", "role": "<wholevar>", "class": "<shape>"} {"op": "SIZE", "role": "<wholevar>", "class": "<size>"} {"op": "SURFACE", "role": "<wholevar>", " class": "<surface>"} ## ROLE SEMANTICS - "whole" = the entire object. - "x" / "y" / "z" = named parts introduced by PART. ## CLASS VALUES Use concise canonical terms describing STATIC PHYSICAL structure (composition, geometry, size, surface texture). ## HARD RULES - NEVER use affordance verbs or their adjective forms (forbidden: "cuttable", "reflective", " absorbent"). - NEVER use dynamic behavior words (forbidden: "compressible", "foldable"); describe the static property that enables the behavior instead (e. g. "foam" not "compressible"). - Every PART must introduce fresh variable name. - Every atom's "role" must be "whole" or previously declared PART variable (no free variables). ## OUTPUT JSON rule only. No markdown, no prose. Validator. The validator labels each atom of the current rule YES/NO for given object. For each (op, class) atom, decide YES/NO: does the given object have this STATIC PHYSICAL property, based on the raw evidence plus your world knowledge of the object? PART-level atom (e.g. SHAPE=sharp_edge) holds if ANY part of the object has the property -- not only the whole. E. g. knife has SHAPE=sharp_edge because its blade does. ## OUTPUT (JSON array) [{"op": "<OP>", "class": "<val>", "holds": true false}, ...] ## OUTPUT FORMAT JSON array only. No markdown, no prose. Auditor. The auditor checks the value of each predicate and updates new ones into the vocabulary. For each proposed class value (none of them literally equals any vocab word -- that's been pre-filtered), decide: - SYNONYM: near-exact semantic equivalence with vocab word -> map to that word. - NEW: genuinely new concept, no clear synonym in vocab -> mapped_to = null. ## OP: {op_name} ## VOCABULARY: {vocab_for_this_op} ## OUTPUT (JSON array) [{"proposed": "<val>", "decision": "SYNONYMNEW", "mapped_to": "<vocab_word>null"}, ...] ## HARD RULE SYNONYM requires the SAME underlying concept within this op's semantic dimension. When unsure, output NEW. ## OUTPUT FORMAT JSON array only. No markdown, no prose. A.2 Rule Examples We present two rules to illustrate KARIs tree structure. pierce_through (Listing 1) shows three alternative physical pathways, each scoped to specific part. fold_flat (Listing 2) shows four alternatives that all describe the whole object. Listing 1: Rule for pierce_through. OR( PART(x, AND(SHAPE: pointed, MADE_OF: rigid_material, SIZE: thin)), PART(x, AND(SHAPE: tapered, MADE_OF: metal)), PART(x, AND(SHAPE: cylindrical, MADE_OF: metal, SIZE: long)) 13 ) OR( Listing 2: Rule for fold_flat. AND(MADE_OF: flexible_material, SHAPE: flat, SURFACE: smooth), AND(MADE_OF: flexible_material, SHAPE: rectangular, SIZE: thin), AND(MADE_OF: flexible_material, SHAPE: cylindrical), AND(MADE_OF: flexible_material, SHAPE: curved, SIZE: broad) )

sections.title: B Experiment Setup Details
sections.content: B.1 Fix20Q Baseline Question Selection The Fixed-Q baseline asks 20 yes/no questions in fixed order, curated from question produced by all evaluated LLM questioners on the test split  (Table 2)  . B.2 Prompts Questioner. The Questioner plays the active role of the game and asks yes/no questions about the hidden objects static physical properties. You are playing 20 Questions game about object AFFORDANCES. "Is it used for cutting?" "Does it absorb liquid?" "Is it tool?" multiple properties at once (non-atomic): "Is it both metal and pointed?" "Is it flexible and smooth?" When you are confident (or after 20 questions), output your final guess using EXACTLY this format on its own line: FINAL_GUESS: <affordance label> where <affordance label> must be one of the candidates above. Output format each turn: QUESTION: <your yes/no question> Oracle. The Oracle sees the full description of the hidden object and replies with single yes/no per question. You are the Oracle in 20 Questions game about physical objects. You know the hidden object described below. Your task is to answer yes/no questions about the object's physical properties -- shape, material, size, surface, structural parts, and other observable physical characteristics. Rules: - Answer ONLY with "Yes" or "No" (one word). - The description below is provided as supplementary context to One of the following affordance labels describes identify which object you are reasoning about. the hidden object. Your goal is to identify it by asking yes/no questions about the object's static physical properties. - Do NOT reveal the object's name. Hidden object description: <object_description> Candidate affordances: 1. <affordance_1> 2. <affordance_2> ... Ask questions along these FOUR physical dimensions: - MATERIAL -- what it is made of (composition, rigidity/flexibility, fibrousness) - SHAPE -- geometric outline, parts, OR topology Checker. The Checker classifies each question into one or more DIMENSION:value pairs and rejects questions that probe function, use, or category. Output is one pair per line, used by the game loop to enforce question atomicity. Classify the question and list EVERY DIMENSION: value pair the question EXPLICITLY probes, one per line. Do NOT infer, expand, (hollow / solid / enclosed / paraphrase, or add synonyms -- output only what layered) the question - SIZE -- dimensions (how big / long / thick) - SURFACE -- outer surface texture literally mentions, using the question's own wording for the value. Rules: - Ask ONE yes/no question per turn, about Dimensions (with one example each): MATERIAL - composition, rigidity/flexibility, EXACTLY ONE property from EXACTLY ONE dimension above. - Do NOT ask directly which affordance it is. - Do NOT ask about the object's function, use, purpose, activity, or category. BAD questions to AVOID: function / use / category: fibrousness "Is it made of metal?" -> MATERIAL: metal "Is it rigid?" -> MATERIAL:rigid SHAPE - geometric outline, the existence of geometric part, OR internal/whole-body topology ( hollow, solid, enclosed, layered, ...). 14 # Dimension Property Count (%) Question 1 Material 2 Surface 3 Material 4 Material 5 Shape 6 Material 7 Shape 8 Shape 9 Material 10 Shape 11 Material 12 Material 13 Shape 14 Size 15 Shape 16 Surface 17 Material 18 Shape 19 Shape 20 Shape rigid smooth metal flexible hollow fabric cylindrical hollow plastic flat wood fibrous elongated thin rectangular rough glass layered bowl-shaped solid 28107 (7.5) 21613 (5.8) 18816 (5.0) 18248 (4.9) 17952 (4.8) 13436 (3.6) 11157 (3.0) 10068 (2.7) 9442 (2.5) 7172 (1.9) 6375 (1.7) 6047 (1.6) 5969 (1.6) 5831 (1.6) 5593 (1.5) 5500 (1.5) 5026 (1.3) 4159 (1.1) 4123 (1.1) 4033 (1.1) Is the object made of rigid material? Is the objects surface smooth? Is the object made of metal? Is the object made of flexible material? Is the object hollow? Is the object made of fabric? Is the objects shape cylindrical? Is the object hollow inside? Is the object made of plastic? Is the object flat? Is the object made of wood? Is the object made of fibrous material? Is the objects shape elongated? Is the object thin? Is the objects shape rectangular? Is the objects surface rough? Is the object made of glass? Is the object layered? Is the objects shape bowl-shaped? Is the object solid? Table 2: The 20 most frequent physical-property questions across all evaluation games, with their targeted dimension and count (percentage of the 373,060 yes/no turns). "Is it cylindrical?" -> SHAPE: cylindrical "Does it have sharp edge?" -> SHAPE:sharp_edge "Is it hollow?" -> SHAPE:hollow SIZE - dimension (how big / long / thick / small) "Is it pocket-sized?" -> SIZE: pocket_sized SURFACE - outer surface texture "Is it smooth?" -> SURFACE:smooth FUNCTION - use / purpose / activity / what the object does "Is it used for cutting?" -> FUNCTION:cutting CATEGORY - class / type / what kind of object it is "Is it tool?" -> CATEGORY:tool Comparative SIZE -- preserve the comparison direction and reference object. Do NOT collapse comparison into canonical size word. "Is it larger than hand?" -> SIZE: larger_than_hand "Is it smaller than coin?" -> SIZE: smaller_than_coin Single vs multi mapping: "Is it long?" -> SIZE:long "Is it long and narrow?" -> SIZE:long SIZE:narrow "Is it made of metal and shiny?" -> MATERIAL: metal SURFACE:shiny Output one DIMENSION:value pair per line. No extra text. B.

sections.title: Implementation Details
sections.content: We evaluate 15 LLM questioners. Open-source models (Llama-3.1-8B, Ministral-8B, Qwen38B/14B, Qwen3.5-9B, Nemotron-9B, Gemma-312B, Phi-4-14B) are served locally with sglang on 8 NVIDIA RTX A6000 GPUs (48 GB each). Closed-source models (GPT-5, GPT5-MINI, GEMINI-2.5-FLASH, GEMINI-2.5-PRO, MINIMAX-M2.5) and the two DeepSeek-V4 variants are accessed through commercial APIs (OpenAI, Google AI Studio, OpenRouter). All decoding uses temperature 0, with per-call max_tokens budget of 512 for the Questioner, 20 for the Oracle, and 50 for the Checker. The Oracle and the Checker are both instances of QWEN3-14B served locally by the same sglang backend. Each game runs to hard cap of 20 turns, and forced guess is requested when the budget is exhausted or when the Checker rejects three consecutive non-atomic questions. B.4 Oracle Validation To verify that the Oracle is accurate enough to drive the game loop, we collect 300 yes/no questions sampled from the test set and have three human annotators label the ground-truth answer for each. We then run three candidate Oracle models on the same set and report agreement with the human labels in Table 3. 15 QWEN3-8B QWEN3-14B GPT-5 Accuracy (%) 85.2 93.4 95.5 Table 3: Oracle agreement with human labels on 300 sampled questions. Affordance Top 5 abrade_surface conduct_heat enclose_substance store_liquid transmit_light Bottom 5 hang_from_above float_on_water hold_between ignite sink_in_water SR 57.8 55.2 53.3 51.9 51.4 10.6 7.2 6.7 4.9 3.8 Table 4: Easiest and hardest affordances, averaged over all 15 vanilla questioners.

sections.title: C Success Rate Breakdown
sections.content: We report the top-5 easiest and bottom-5 hardest affordances for three model groups: 1) All 15 LLMs  (Table 4)  , 2) Open-source LLMs  (Table 5)  , and 3) Closed-source LLMs  (Table 6)  .

sections.title: D Human Annotation
sections.content: D.1 Participants and Instructions. We recruited annotators for four roles: (1) Five volunteers who played the human baseline on the 30% subset. The instructions are the same as Questioner (Section B.2). (2) Three annotators who verified the Oracle answers on sampled 300-question subset reported in Table 3. The instructions are the same as Oracle (Section B.2). (3,4) Six annotators were involved in our manual annotation and refinement process in Stages 2 and 3 of AFFORDANCE20Qcollection and annotation. Three additional annotators verified our labels for the object-affordance pair. ALL instructions as follows: Affordance Top 5 abrade_surface store_liquid transmit_light conduct_heat enclose_substance Bottom 5 hang_from_above float_on_water ignite hold_between sink_in_water SR 73.3 70.4 65.7 63.8 60.9 9.1 8.7 4.3 2.9 2.6 Table 5: Easiest and hardest affordances for the top-5 open-source models (DEEPSEEK-V4-FLASH, DEEPSEEK-V4-PRO, GEMMA-3-12B, QWEN3-14B, PHI-4-14B). Affordance Top 5 bounce_back enclose_substance pierce_through store_liquid abrade_surface Bottom 5 sweep hold_between ignite float_on_water sink_in_water SR 71.4 70.4 62.6 61.7 60.0 13.3 11.4 9.6 7.8 7. Table 6: Easiest and hardest affordances for the top-5 closed-source models (GEMINI-2.5-PRO, GEMINI-2.5FLASH, GPT-5, MINIMAX-M2.5, GPT-5-MINI). characteristics, dimensions, and structural parts. Guidelines: - Focus strictly on physical and geometric properties. - Do not include functional, social, or usagerelated descriptions. - Materials should be written as material names only (e.g., metal, wood, plastic, stainless steel). - Surface and shape attributes should describe only physical appearance or geometry. - Part-specific properties should be assigned to the corresponding object parts rather than the global object description. - The text description should contain 3-- You are participating in an annotation task to sentences describing construct structured only physical characteristics. physical descriptions for everyday objects. - Do not include phrases such as "used for", " Your goal is to describe each object using only observable physical attributes, including shape, material composition, surface designed to", or "can be used". - Global size should reflect realistic dimensions in centimeters. 16 Please provide the final annotation in JSON format. You are participating in an annotation task for physical affordance benchmark. For each listed object, determine which affordances the object physically supports based only on its observable properties, including shape, geometry, material, surface characteristics, and structural components. An affordance should be assigned only if the object's physical properties reasonably satisfy the affordance definition. Guidelines: - Base decisions only on visible or physically inferable properties. - Do not consider electronic, chemical, or mechanism-specific functions unless they are directly implied by the object's physical structure. - If an affordance clearly applies, include it; if uncertain, leave it unassigned. Please provide annotations in the following JSON format: [ ] {"object": "<name>", "has": ["<affordance_name >", ...]}, ... D.2 Recruitment and payment. All participants were university students who voluntarily participated through internal recruitment channels. Participants did not receive any financial compensation. D.3 Data Consent All participants were informed that their annotations would be used solely for academic research and dataset construction purposes prior to participation. And no demographic or personally identifying information was retained beyond the annotation outputs.
